title: MacIntyre's Texas Crown: Scot Bids for Third PGA Tour Title
excerpt: Robert MacIntyre enters the final round of the Valero Texas Open with a four-shot lead, chasing his third PGA Tour title and a crucial Masters appearance.
body:
MacIntyre's Moment at TPC San Antonio

Scotland's Robert MacIntyre arrived at the Valero Texas Open with something to prove — and through 54 holes, he's made his case crystal clear. After an imperious second round that saw him post a 64 to take a commanding four-shot lead into Sunday's finale, MacIntyre is positioned for his third PGA Tour victory. What's more, a win would secure him a final last-minute spot in next week's Masters at Augusta National, the game's most prestigious tournament.

The Scot's 36-hole score of 130 is the lowest recorded at TPC San Antonio since 2010, signalling intent and precision when it matters most. In front of him, the field is hungry — Ludvig Aberg, Michael Kim, Ryo Hisatsune and Matt Wallace all licking their lips at the possibility of making up ground and claiming the trophy themselves.

MacIntyre's Lead
-4
Score Through 54 Holes
-14
MacIntyre's 36-Hole Score
130
Record Since 2010
Lowest

History on the Line

Should MacIntyre prevail on Sunday, he would become the first player since Zach Johnson in 2009 to successfully defend the Texas Open title. But there's more intrigue: he'd be the third consecutive left-handed golfer to lift the Valero trophy. Akshay Bhatia won in 2024, Brian Harman claimed it in 2025, and a MacIntyre victory would make PGA Tour history — the first instance of three straight southpaws winning a single event.

Weather and Grinding

Saturday's heavy rains suspended play before MacIntyre could finish his third round, with only six holes completed. Preferred lies were in effect on Sunday as torrential downpours continued to lash San Antonio. The delayed third round resumed early Sunday morning, with both MacIntyre and his closest pursuers needing to navigate the soggy Oaks Course.

The Masters Puzzle

For players outside the traditional qualification categories, the Valero Texas Open winner secures an automatic Masters invite. Matt Wallace sits in third place and eyeing that dream week at Augusta. So too are Ryo Hisatsune, Michael Kim and a handful of others. Should any pull off the upset, they'd be joining the game's elite at golf's most hallowed grounds. But with MacIntyre's four-shot cushion and pedigree, expect him to hold sway as Sunday unfolds.

The Masters Next Week

Win or lose, the first major of 2026 awaits. Rory McIlroy returns as defending champion, with Scottie Scheffler installed as favourite. But next Sunday evening, one golfer — likely the Scot from North Berwick — will have one more Texan treasure locked away and a one-way ticket to Augusta.
